exemplar.title: An investigation of the multi-store memory model on the recency effect

detailedExemplarData.title: An investigation of the multi-store memory model on the recency effect

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear and relevant investigation aim with explicit explanation of relevance to Grade-11 learners
Accurate description of the multi-store memory model establishing theoretical foundation
Fully operationalised hypotheses with clear identification of IV and DV
weaknesses: Theoretical model description could be more deeply integrated with the hypothesis to improve logical flow
Limited critical contextâexpansion on alternative memory frameworks would strengthen depth
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The introduction is strong: the aim is clearly stated and its relevance to study habits is well explained, and hypotheses are fully operationalised. However, while the MSM is accurately described, the link to the hypothesis is not as seamlessly integrated and the broader theoretical context could be deepened. This places the work solidly in the mid-upper band but not at the top, meriting 4/6.

strengths: Use of an independent-measures design is well explained and justified (reducing order effects)
Sampling technique and participant characteristics are clearly described and rationalised
Controlled variables and materials are explicitly justified to isolate the IV
weaknesses: Discussion of volunteer bias and its effect on internal validity is limited
Ethical procedures (e.g., debriefing) lack full detail
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Exploration is competent and meets the âexplainedâ descriptors across all strands. Justifications for design, sampling, and materials demonstrate clear understanding. Minor gaps in addressing volunteer bias and fuller ethical detail prevent awarding full marks, so a score of 3/4 is appropriate.

strengths: Appropriate and accurate use of median, IQR, and Mann-Whitney U test with correct reporting
Interpretation of statistical findings is linked to the hypothesis and conceptualised in terms of interference
weaknesses: Graphical presentation has technical weaknesses (minimal axis labels, bar median not ideal for IQR data)
Omitted effect-size calculation and outlier-identification criteria
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Analysis demonstrates strong statistical choice and accurate reporting, as well as clear interpretation linked to the hypothesis. However, graphical execution has errors and key details like effect size and outlier methods are missing. These shortcomings lower the performance to 3/6.

strengths: Strengths and limitations are clearly stated, explained, and directly linked to internal/external validity
Modifications are explicitly linked to identified limitations and justified
weaknesses: Findings discussion is largely descriptive with limited critical analysis of theoretical implications
Alternative models (e.g., working memory framework) are not considered in the evaluation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Evaluation shows solid strengths/limitations and well-justified modifications, but the discussion of findings against theory remains surface-level and lacks critical depth. The failure to engage alternative theoretical perspectives reduces the score to 2/6.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar graph displaying medians with error bars is present and addresses the hypothesis. However, axis labels are minimal and the graph format (bar of median) is not the most informative for IQR data, indicating some technical weakness; hence mid-band (3-4) with a 3 is appropriate.

Findings are explicitly linked to statistics (U = 36.5, p < .05) and the null hypothesis is rejected. The student explains what the direction means for recall and ties it to interference, satisfying interpretation criteria. Some conceptual confusion with MSM prevents the very top mark.

Both null and alternative hypotheses are given, the IV (presence/absence of 30-s distraction) and DV (number of last-10 words recalled) are **fully operationalised**. No ambiguities are present, so the strand achieves the maximum within the top band.

Sampling (self-selected via social media) is identified and **rationalised in terms of motivation/generalizability**; participant characteristics are outlined (age, grade, common language). This meets the requirement for explanation rather than simple description.

The multi-store memory model is **accurately described** (three separate stores, capacity/duration limits, role of rehearsal). The student also **explains how the model underpins the current investigation** (recency as STM trace, delay causing decay). Minor wording inaccuracies aside, the conceptual connection is clear and correct, meriting the upper band, but the description is not especially detailed to score a 6.

The student relates results to both Glanzer & Cunitz and the multi-store model, noting consistencies and discrepancies. The discussion is descriptive and fairly surface-level; deeper critical analysis of theoretical implications is limited, placing it in the 3-4 band.

The student clearly states the aim (to investigate the effect of a 30-s distraction on the recency portion of the serial-position curve) and **explicitly explains why this is relevant** for Grade-11 learners (links to study habits and optimisation of learning). The explanation is brief but sufficient and goes beyond a mere identification; therefore a solid placement in the 5-6 band is justified, though the depth is not exceptional enough to warrant the very top of the band.

Multiple strengths and limitations are **explained in relation to internal/external validity and linked back to distraction effect**, demonstrating relevance. The explanation is clear though not exhaustive enough for a 6.

Controlled variables (word length, presentation time, language) and materials (standardised 30-word list, distraction task) are not only listed but **explicitly justified as necessary to isolate the IV**. This fulfils the âexplainedâ criterion.

Each stated limitation is followed by a **specific, justified modification** (matched-pairs design, stratified sampling, complex materials). The links are explicit and reasoning is sound, fulfilling the top-band descriptor though justification depth is moderate rather than extensive.

The student not only describes but **explains why an independent-measures design with random allocation was used** (avoid order effects, isolate distraction). The justification shows understanding, satisfying the âexplainedâ descriptor; explanation depth is competent but not exceptionally sophisticated.

Appropriate descriptive (median/IQR) and inferential (Mann-Whitney U) statistics are used and correctly reported (U, critical value, p). No computational errors are evident. Minor omissions such as effect size prevent a perfect 6 but accuracy and appropriateness justify a high score in the 5-6 band.

annotations.comment: The investigation aim is clearly articulated and its relevance to Grade 11 learners is well explained, linking how distractions and rehearsal strategies impact study habits.
The hypotheses include both null and alternative statements and correctly identify independent and dependent variables, demonstrating full operationalisation.
Consider integrating the theoretical model description more closely with the hypothesis to strengthen the logical flow between theory and research design.
The independent-measures design is well explained and justified, showing clear understanding of how random allocation minimizes order effects and isolates the distraction variable.
Participant demographics and sampling via social media are described and rationalised in terms of motivation and generalisability, enhancing the exploration of sampling methods.
Highlighting the controlled uniformity of the word list to reduce extraneous variability strengthens the relevance of method limitations and robustness discussion.
Proposing stratified sampling to include diverse cultural backgrounds directly addresses generalizability and links to identified sampling limitation.
The suggestion to use complex narratives for future studies is well justified and clearly addresses the limitation of low ecological validity.

The student provides an accurate and concise description of the multi-store memory model, clearly outlining sensory, short-term, and long-term stores. This establishes a strong theoretical foundation for the investigation.
The summary of Glanzer and Cunitzâs classic study is clear, but expanding on its methodological limitations or alternative memory frameworks would deepen the introductionâs critical context.
Could the student more explicitly link the described model directly to the current research aim and hypothesis in this section to improve clarity?
The internal validity of the independent-measures design is addressed clearly, showing how practice effects are minimised and results attributed to the distraction manipulation.
While self-selected sampling is justified, discussing potential volunteer bias and its effect on internal validity would strengthen the sampling critique.
Materials are standardised and controlled variables are clearly outlined (word length, presentation time, language), demonstrating thorough planning to isolate the independent variable.
Elaborate on why a 30-word two-syllable list was chosen specifically to balance cognitive load and linguistic variability within the STM task.
Ethical considerations like deception are explained, but the debriefing process could be detailed further to demonstrate full participant protection and transparency.
Selecting median and IQR for descriptive statistics is appropriate given ratio-level data and the presence of an outlier, reflecting good statistical decision-making.
The criteria for identifying outliers are not specified; including the method used (e.g., 1.5ÃIQR rule) would clarify the decision process for data cleaning.
Table 1 presents medians and IQRs clearly for each condition, facilitating an effective comparison of central tendency and variability.
Add labels or footnotes to specify that the IQR represents the middle 50% of scores, improving self-containment of the table.
The interpretation of the variability (IQR) differences is insightful, correctly linking dispersion to consistency of participantsâ recall performance.
The bar graph using medians does not best represent IQR data. Consider replacing with a box-and-whisker plot to directly display median, quartiles, and outliers.
The choice of the Mann-Whitney U test is well justified by the data level and distribution, and U-value reporting against critical value supports statistical conclusions.
Including an effect size (e.g., r or Î·Â²) would enhance interpretation of the practical significance of the difference.
The discussion effectively compares findings with original Glanzer & Cunitz results and the multi-store model, highlighting consistencies and novel observations.
The theoretical discussion remains descriptive; incorporate critical analysis of alternative models (e.g., working memory framework) to deepen evaluation.
Acknowledging volunteer motivation due to self-selection and its impact on data quality demonstrates a strong understanding of sampling influences on validity.
When discussing the matched-pairs modification, specify matching variables (e.g., baseline memory scores) and how pairs would be allocated to control individual differences.
The link between using complex materials and improved ecological validity could be further elaborated, explaining how narratives engage different memory processes.
Consider concluding with a concise summary of how findings inform revision of the multi-store model and implications for educational practice to strengthen closure.
